data.title: Liberty Global: Making Sense Of The Valuation
data.description:
On the paper, Liberty Global's (NASDAQ:LBTYA), (NASDAQ:LBTYK), (NASDAQ:LBTYB) management presents a $25+ SOTP (Sum-Of-The-Parts) valuation. Reality gives investors a check with a price of $10.5. The market clearly looks at the company through different lenses.

There is the SOTP lens of $25, where Liberty's assets, cash pile, and investments total way above today's share price. Then, there is the free cash flow (FCF) lens, where the market sees the company that just spun off its highest cash-generating asset (Sunrise), leaving it with limited FCF and an unclear strategy for near-term shareholder returns.

There are two ways to move the valuation up toward the SOTP:

  1. Aggressive buybacks or monetizations that prove SOTP is being prioritized.

  2. Normalized FCF growth as capex cycles roll off and high-leverage assets generate cash.

So, Liberty’s future valuation depends on either shifting market perception from the FCF lens to the SOTP lens or improvement in FCF. In this article, I'm going to look at the company from both perspectives, assess the position of each Telco's potential FCF, and discuss what needs to happen for the market to switch the valuation from FCF to SOTP.

This article is not an introduction to Liberty Global's complex business. If you are new to the thesis, I suggest you start with my previous Liberty articles.

Liberty's Current Structure

To understand how to value Liberty, we must start with how it sees itself. Liberty describes its business across three platforms.

Liberty Telecom: European telcos in the UK, Netherlands, Belgium, and Ireland

Liberty Growth: Portfolio of media, tech, and infra investments

Liberty Services: Centralized tech/finance platforms with long-term contracts to Liberty's telcos and potential contracts with the companies from their Growth portfolio.

SOTP Valuation vs. Market Price

As Liberty's management presented their SOTP valuation, they arrived at $25/share when assuming a 1x multiple of MC/EBITDA for their Telcos.

Just 1 additional EBITDA multiple more than doubles our stock. - Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

On paper, this checks out. Cash, Liberty Growth, and proportional telecom EBITDA support such a valuation. However, the market disagrees with valuing Liberty based on SOTP but rather on FCF, which is currently depressed without Sunrise.

To close this gap between these valuations, Liberty needs visible catalysts: spin-offs, IPOs, and especially buybacks. Until then, SOTP is a theoretical upside, not a market reality.

Strategic Plan for 2025: Re-Rating the HoldCo

…spin-offs. Although as many of you know, this decision is dependent on several tax, legal and financial factors. We could also consider tracking stocks, IPOs. And of course, we always remain open to M&A options. — Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

While not guaranteed, Liberty laid out a strategic roadmap for 2025:

  • Monetize $500M–$750M in non-core assets
  • Buy back up to 10% of shares (I wish there were more. In previous years, Liberty announced increased share buybacks in the middle of the year, so there is still hope for an increase.)
  • Secure recurring fees from OpCos via Liberty Services (More about it later)
  • Continue exploring IPOs, spin-offs, or tracking stocks

Shareholders were already rewarded by the $10 Sunrise (SNRE) spin-off last year, which has proven the concept works well. Tracking stocks, on the other side, deserves a closer look. John Malone (Liberty's spiritual godfather) has used trackers frequently. Historical examples clearly show that trackers usually trade at a discount unless used only as a short-term bridge to a real spin-off. Creating new trackers would likely tend to reinforce the Holdco discount, not resolve it.

The Road to Free Cash Flow Normalization

For the current year, FCF from Telcos is estimated at £350-400M for VMO2, €300M for VodafoneZiggo, and €180-150M negative for Telenet.

The biggest reason the FCF looks weak right now is the capex cycle. With Sunrise gone, Liberty’s other telco assets need to step up.

The telco's markets can also be brutal, with alt-nets changing the comfortable duopoly/oligopoly markets into a furious fight for each client, with FCF margins nearing zero.

UK (VMO2)

50% ownership in Virgin Media O2 is Liberty’s most mature asset. Capex is stable around at ~£2.1B, driven by fiber buildouts via nexfibre.

…we're generating free cash at VMO2 and VodafoneZiggo as we have in the past and as we expect to in the future…” — Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

This is a reliable, cash-producing engine that can support buybacks, especially as nexfibre penetration grows. It is questionable when the capex goes down, but as I stated in my previous analysis, we could potentially see up to £1B FCF three years out. For that to happen, the market needs to get more rational. If not, even in a lower capex environment, we could still see £300M FCF.

Netherlands (VodafoneZiggo)

VodafoneZiggo continues to be a strong FCF contributor. Capex guidance remains in line, and the 50% joint venture is expected to distribute €300M in 2025 again. I consider it a dependable, low-drama cash generator.

Belgium (Telenet + Wyre)

Here’s where capex bites. 2025 is a peak Capex year for both the ServCo (Telenet) and the fiber NetCo (Wyre). Belgium is temporarily skewing group capex upward. Crucially, Liberty isn’t paying for this expansion:

We also use the bank market to finance large CapEx builds. We just agreed a EUR 500 million stand-alone CapEx facility for Wyre. We expect capital intensity in the Telenet ServCo to decline in 2026 as the major investments in the mobile network will be completed in 2025, while CapEx will also be fully debt financed… meaning no equity requirement from Liberty Global or Telenet.— Charlie Bracken, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

This delays FCF from Belgium but also protects Liberty shareholders from dilution or downside. Already in 2026 and beyond, this market could go from a drag to a meaningful €200-300M in FCF.

Telco's Sum Of FCF

To sum it up, counting depressed £375M*50% from VMO2, €300M*50% from VodafoneZiggo, €250M from Telenet, negative $200M for central costs, we arrive at $300M FCF. If we see the improvement from VMO2 after the Capex goes down in 2028, the FCF could rise even above $800M.

$300M and $800M are very different sums, but as I say, it's hard to estimate as it's dependent on the markets getting more rational.

With a Market Cap of $3.7B, this gives us a 12x - 4.6x FCF multiple. This isn't crazy cheap, but don’t forget there is still over $3B in Growth portfolio and $2.2B in cash.

Why does SOTP deserve more weight in the valuation?

The simple answer is the genius corporate structure with the debt and now Liberty Services.

The Debt Structure

The debt sits at the operating level, not at the parent. Each asset carries its leverage, and none of it is recourse to Liberty. The debt is huge, but the HoldCo itself is $2.2B net cash.

This structure provides two key advantages:

  1. If a telco underperforms, Liberty’s exposure is capped.
  2. If a telco is monetized, Liberty captures the full equity upside.

Liberty Services: The Silent Value Sucker

In parallel, Liberty quietly reshaped its corporate structure. "Liberty Services" — which includes "Liberty Tech" and "Liberty Blume" now generates ~$600M in recurring revenue, with contracts signed directly with Liberty’s own telcos and, in the future, with companies from their "Growth Portfolio".

This matters. By shifting costs to Liberty Services, the company creates recurring, contractual cash flow at the HoldCo level.

That means that if Liberty spins off any asset, it still retains service revenues through multi-year agreements. This structure essentially sucks cash flow from their assets and helps preserve value at the top, regardless of how the underlying assets evolve with their isolated debt burdens.

Liberty Growth Portfolio

Liberty Growth includes dozens of stakes. But 75% of this portfolio value is concentrated in 7 companies.

My take:

  • Infrastructure holdings like AtlasEdge and EdgeConnex are valuable and potentially monetizable.
  • Media stakes (Formula E, TelevisaUnivision, ITV) are harder to value and could become distractions. I wasn't happy when Liberty increased its stake in Formula E.

Looking at the depressed valuation, it's clear that investors don’t want Liberty using its cash pile to expand Liberty Growth.

Buybacks or another Deal

Buybacks are the most obvious way to close the SOTP gap. And Liberty is trying - $700M in 2024, another 10% of shares targeted in 2025.

I would argue that 10% buybacks didn't improve the valuation in the past and the company should be more aggressive. But, instead of accelerating repurchases, Liberty has continued to increase Capex and considering more money spent on growth. Rumors of acquiring the rest of VodafoneZiggo hit the market. When the news broke, LBTYA dropped 10% in pre-market. It opened flat, but the message was clear: shareholders want monetization, not expansion.

Conclusion

I consider Liberty's structure complex but elegant. A business that captures all potential upside but is isolated from the risk of default on any of its assets.

On SOTP basis, the shares are deeply undervalued. However, the market refuses to assign that value unless it sees more proof of monetization, buybacks, or rising FCF.

That proof is coming — capex cycles are peaking, isolated debt protects the HoldCo and Liberty Services are quietly shifting value upward. Ultimately, the capital allocation will determine whether all of this pays off.

If Liberty pushes harder into buybacks and spins, the stock should re-rate. If it leans deeper into growth, the return is more questionable.

For now, I am taking advantage of the huge valuation asymmetry and assigning a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Ethereum: A Deep Dive In Network Activity
data.description:
Introduction

In this article, we will analyse what has happened on the Ethereum (ETH-USD) network and what implications these events have had on the price level. We will also provide a future outlook for the asset price, supported by data and the new Pectra update. Ethereum has declined over the last 90 days by 50% to USD 1500, coming from USD 3000 and higher levels. Its last ATH (All-Time High), located at 4880 USD, dates back to November 10, 2021. This is really strange, as in similar assets we have seen price peaks in much more recent times (See Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or Ripple (XRP-USD) in early 2025).

Looking at it from the point of view of the total market capitalisation of cryptocurrencies, we can see that this value has increased in the last quarter of 2024 by 63.52%. Of this 63.52% increase, 54.77% corresponds to an increase in Bitcoin's market capitalisation, as can be seen in the following image. Consequently, 45.23% or 736.74 billion USD flowed into the so-called altcoins at that time. Considering that Ethereum is the most representative asset of the altcoins, that it did not reach a new high at the beginning of this cycle seems surprising. I will try to put a face to the reason for this.

Analysis: Network View

TVL

We can see in the following graph the evolution of the TVL (Total Value Locked) in a native asset, deployed in the Ethereum network, over time. It can be seen that the TVL is at a peak, even though the price has fallen in recent months. To me, this is a good sign, as there continues to be a strong commitment from validators to block/stake their coins to validate transactions and secure the network. We also note the green and red bars, which represent monthly increases/decreases in LTV greater than or equal to 15%. It is quite noticeable that when such increases/decreases occur, the price rises or falls accordingly. So this variable represents an important bid attraction and bid blocking mechanism. The more ETH there is staked, the less liquid supply is available on the exchanges and the more likely there is to be a supply contraction caused by an increase in demand.

Daily Active Addresses - App Activity

At this point, we analyse the evolution of the active addresses of the Ethereum network, disaggregated by applications. In the shape of the graph, we can see a clear harmony between the growth of addresses and the growth of the price. The Pearson statistic explains this at 0.8. While it is true that the price of the asset has decreased, the active addresses have remained fairly stable in recent times, with some downward variations.

Therefore, we also analyse what happens when there are monthly increases/decreases of more than 20% in the daily active addresses. We see that when there is an increase of this magnitude, the price generally follows the addresses. This is mainly because these new addresses demand the native asset in order to be able to perform the operations they want on the network itself. From my point of view, I think this is an important variable to take into account and monitor (it can be done in applications such as Artemis Crypto), as it clearly shows us the real use of the network.

Transactions - App Activity

Another of the variables we analysed is the number of transactions carried out on the Ethereum network, represented in a disaggregated way by category of applications. As with the active addresses variable, from the shape of the graph, it seems that when the number of transactions carried out on the network increases, the price of the asset also increases. This is because the two variables have a correlation of 0.81 using Pearson's correlation coefficient.

This second graph represents the set of total transactions vs. price over time. Reflecting monthly increases of more than 15% in the number of transactions in the green bars and decreases of the same size in the red bars. In some phases, increased transactions are accompanied by an increase in price, which we determined to be an important variable to monitor. In the end, the pairing of active addresses and transactions reflects user interest and activity in any given network.

Gas Consumed (USD) - App Activity

In this section, we analyse the variable that I consider most important, the gas or fees in USD generated in aggregate by the total number of applications running on the Ethereum network, which corresponds to the revenue of the Ethereum Protocol. As the reader may recall, on 15 July 2022 Ethereum changed its consensus mechanism from Proof of Work (PoW) to Proof of Stake (PoS). One of the main reasons for this change was to be able to lower transaction fees (Gas) to make the network more competitive and thus attract users who will compensate for this reduction. Well, as can be seen in the following graph, this has not been fulfilled in any way. The level of users has remained almost constant and the protocol fees have been drastically reduced. This is also due to the emergence of layer 2 networks that take advantage of Ethereum's infrastructure (EVM) without ‘paying’ the fees that would be fair at the moment. I believe that Ethereum needs to achieve a fair level of revenue for everyone who has an indispensable need to use its technology infrastructure. Right now, it is failing to do so and that is why there has been a drop in its price.

Below, we can see the total gas consumed by all the applications running on the Ethereum network. As can be seen, the historical maximum of Ethereum coincides with the historical maximum in terms of Gas Consumed, this is completely normal, as it reflects pure demand for the protocol. It can also be seen that the vast majority of green bars (monthly increases in gas consumption of more than 70%) coincide with local price peaks. It is quite possible that Ethereum will achieve this with the new ‘Pectra’ update, which will be implemented on 7 May, but it is only a possibility.

Conclusion

In this last graph, we analyse the historical price of Ethereum vs the percentage of the circulating supply, which is in staking, blocked by the different validators of the network. We do this because I think it can help us to unravel the future. As we discussed earlier, on 7 May 2025, in just under a month, the Ethereum network will implement Pectra, the most important upgrade since Dencun (2024). One of the relevant points of this update is EIP-7251 which states; "This EIP increases the maximum balance of validators from 32 ETH to 2048 ETH. This allows validators to staking larger amounts more efficiently. It also reduces the number of validators needed, which decreases the network load and improves the efficiency of the blockchain." As can be seen in the chart below, if validators start depositing 2048 ETH instead of 32, it could result in a circulating supply contraction. Doing some quick maths 2048 is 64 times more than 32, which could raise the percentage of staking locked asset over circulating supply, which could translate into a price increase of the asset.

In the short term, after the successful implementation of Pectra, I believe Ethereum has the potential to increase in value. For the medium term, the protocol should be able to better capture revenue from anyone using its technology stack.

data.title: U.S. Office Market Report: Office Valuations Remain Under Pressure
data.description:
The office sector continues to face difficulties, but there is a distinct separation in performance between the amenity-rich premier office assets and the older, lower-quality buildings where few employees have returned to the office. Low valuations across the office sector, coupled with high interest rates and lenders viewing the sector with greater trepidation, could lead to refinance challenges for landlords with mortgages coming due.

The Take

➤ Office real estate investment trusts continued to trade at large discounts to their estimated net asset value (NAV), and the discount has grown since the Federal Reserve signaled it would be more cautious about cutting interest rates in 2025.

➤ Supply dynamics should prove a favorable tailwind for the office market. Some of the existing older office buildings with little leasing demand are going offline. This is because of factors such as residential conversions or landlords simply choosing not to invest more capital to get the property into a leasable state. At the same time, there is little to no new office construction.

➤ Office property transaction volume increased year-over-year in 2024, but remained low overall, making price discoverability challenging for the sector.

Valuations down for office REITs

Valuations for office real estate investment trusts have consistently fallen since mid-December after the Federal Reserve signaled it would be more hesitant to cut interest rates in 2025. As of March 12, the office REIT sector traded at a 27.3% median discount to NAV, a 12.7-percentage point decline compared to the 14.6% median discount as of the end of November 2024.

Office REIT stocks trade at a much larger discount to NAV compared to the broader REIT market. On a market-cap-weighted basis, the Dow Jones US Real Estate Office index closed March 12 at a 24.2% discount to NAV, while the broader Dow Jones Equity All REIT index closed the same day at a 1.6% market-cap-weighted premium to NAV.

Premier office buildings, the highest-quality of buildings, continue to outperform the broader office market.

BXP Inc.'s CEO Owen Thomas said on the REIT's fourth-quarter 2024 earnings call that the direct vacancy rate of premier office buildings in the five central business district markets where his company operates — Boston, New York, San Francisco, Seattle and Washington, DC — was 13.2%, versus 18.8% for the broader market. CBRE's research defines premier workplaces as the highest-quality 7% of buildings, which represent 13% of total space in those five central business district markets.

The amount of premier office space available for leasing in BXP's markets declined by 8.8 million square feet over the last three years, whereas available space in the broader office market increased by 15.6 million square feet during the same period.

Thomas said asking rents for premier workplaces were also 50% higher than the broader market, up from approximately a 40% premium three years ago.

While there appears to be clear bifurcation between different quality properties, price discovery remains opaque.

Property transactions remained low for all commercial real estate in 2024, including office. Office real estate transactions in the US totaled approximately $58.18 billion in 2024, up 26% from $46.19 billion the year prior, but less than half of the $139.09 billion of office transactions in 2019, according to data from Real Capital Analytics.

Elevated interest rates coupled with significant bid-ask spreads between buyers and sellers have kept many deals on the sidelines for the time being. As more investment properties come to the market, REITs with capital on hand and strong balance sheets may find unique opportunities in the future.

Stress in office loan portfolios remain elevated

Credit quality of office loans remained stressed for many of the top office lenders as of year-end 2024.

In an analysis of banks that reported at least $1 billion in outstanding office loans as of Dec. 31, 2024, Wells Fargo & Co., the largest office loan lender in an analysis, reported that 11.5% of its $27.4 billion office loan portfolio were nonperforming as of 2024 year-end. Bank of America Corp. and PNC Financial Services Group Inc. reported similar levels of nonperforming office loans, at 11% and 12.6%, respectively.

Interest rate hikes over the last three years have made refinancing increasingly difficult for office property owners who originated loans at much lower rates and are now struggling with higher vacancy rates and persistent remote work trends.

Banks continued to hold loan-loss reserves at high-single or low-double digits for loans tied to office properties to help prepare for rough sledding ahead. Banks also claimed that they have low loan-to-value ratios on their office loans, giving them significant equity cushions. While price discovery remains challenging to come by, given the low transaction volume in the marketplace, price declines might have been severe enough to wipe out those cushions, especially for lower-quality office assets.

With elevated stress in the office sector, many of the banks with the largest exposure to the sector are paring back their office loan portfolios and reducing their exposure. In an analysis of 26 banks with more than $1 billion each in office loans, 20 reduced the total balance of their office loan portfolios in the fourth quarter of 2024 compared to the quarter prior.

Outlook for office sector

While leasing activity and demand for high-quality premier office properties remained strong, concerns around office CRE overall are likely to linger for at least a couple more years, with the highest concern around lower-quality office buildings.

While it is still early into President Donald Trump's second term, his planned policy to have federal workers return to in-office work could support the Washington, DC office market. Bringing federal employees back to the office could motivate government-adjacent employees, such as government contractors, to work more from their offices. The additional street life from more employees working from the office would also be an uplift to local retailers and restaurants in the area.

That being said, the Trump administration has also implemented layoffs within the federal workforce, potentially outweighing any gains from their return-to-office efforts for the DC market.

Supply dynamics should also prove a favorable tailwind for the office market in the coming years. A portion of the existing older supply of office buildings with little leasing demand is going offline, while there is little to no new office construction.

Office-to-residential conversion projects continue to gain steam in New York City. On SL Green Realty Corp.'s fourth-quarter 2024 earnings call, the REIT's CEO Marc Holliday categorized the office-to-residential conversion opportunities in the city as "kind of a triple win," and clarified that the conversions take obsolete space off the market, help address the housing crisis and help revitalize city life.

Banks continue to monitor their office loan portfolios, with many setting aside loss reserves for loans that may not be re-financeable in the current environment. Banks are expected to continue to view the asset class with caution and likely will extend new credit with less favorable terms and pricing to borrowers. Many traditional lenders do view CRE loans, including certain smaller office loans, as part of their core competency and should continue to service existing customers.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Corpay: The Bull Case Remains Compelling
data.description:
Shares of Corpay, Inc. (NYSE:CPAY) (previously known as Fleetcor Technologies) have delivered a total return of 3% since my initial Strong Buy recommendation, Fleetcor Technologies: 5 Reasons Why I Am Bullish, was published on January 30, 2024. Comparably, the S&P 500 has delivered a total return of 9% over the same time period.

Corpay's business has performed well, and the growth outlook remains strong. Moreover, the stock's valuation remains compelling. For these reasons, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock and view the recent pullback as a buying opportunity.

Solid Q4 Results

Corpay reported Q4 2024 results on February 5, 2025. Adjusted EPS for the quarter came in at $5.36, up 21% year-over-year, which beat estimates by $0.01. Revenue for the quarter came in at $1.034 billion, which slightly missed consensus estimates. FY 2024 Adjusted EPS came in at $19.01 which represents a 12% increase from FY 2023 levels.

The company's strong growth was driven by its Corporate Payments segment, which delivered 26% quarterly revenue growth on a year-over-year basis. The company's Vehicle Payments segment also performed during the quarter, delivering 8% year-over-year growth. Margin improvement also contributed further to earnings growth as Adjusted EBITDA margins for the quarter improved to 55.2% up from 54.2% during the same period a year ago.

Corpay also updated its outlook for FY 2025. The company expects to deliver revenue and Adjusted EPS growth of 10-12% for FY 2025. The company expects FY 2025 Adjusted EPS to come in at $20.75 - $21.25 per share. Consensus Adjusted EPS estimates for FY 2025 currently stand at $21.05 which is close to the midpoint of company guidance. As shown by the table below, the company has a solid history of delivering earnings which are in line with consensus estimates. One important thing to note is that company guidance was provided prior to the Trump administration's reciprocal tariff announcement and ensuing increased levels of financial market volatility, which have weighed on the macroeconomic outlook. However, it is important to note that China is not a key market for the company, and thus it has less exposure to the ongoing trade dispute between China and the U.S. Moreover, as a payments company, Corpay is well positioned to benefit from potential increases in prices which may help to offset a potential drop in corporate spending due to economic uncertainty.

Investor Friendly Capital Deployment

In my previous piece, I noted that I viewed Corpay's aggressive share repurchase program as a positive. At the time of my prior piece, the company had $700 million remaining under its share repurchase program. During Q3 2024, the company increased its share repurchase authorization by $1 billion and for FY 2024 the company repurchased 4.2 million shares for a total of roughly $1.28 billion. Currently, $1.3 billion remains under the company's repurchase authorization. Based on current market prices, this represents roughly 6% of shares outstanding, up from 3.3% at the time of my prior piece. I view the increasing authorization as a positive as it allows Corpay to repurchase more shares than was previously the case.

In addition to an aggressive share repurchase program, Corpay has also been deploying capital via attractive tuck in acquisitions. In 2024, the company completed acquisitions of GPS, Paymerang, and Zapay for a total of $1.265 billion. Corpay has continued to execute on its acquisition strategy already in 2025 with the announcement that it would acquire Gringo, a leading vehicle registration and compliance payment company in Brazil.

In 2025, the company also plans to use up to $1.5 billion of free cash flow to pay down debt. I view this as a good use of capital as the company's leverage ratio has increased to 2.75x, up from 2.43x as of Q4 2023 due to an increase in debt outstanding. A lower leverage ratio should help reduce the company's exposure to a rising interest rate environment.

Valuation Update

Corpay currently trades at 14.5x consensus FY 2025 Adjusted EPS. Comparably, the broader market trades at roughly 20x consensus forward earnings. At the time of my prior piece, Corpay had been trading at 15x consensus one year forward Adjusted EPS, while the broader market had been trading at 22x consensus forward earnings. Thus, both Corpay and the broader market are trading at a slightly more attractive valuation than was previously the case. While Corpay's valuation discount to the broader market has slightly narrowed, I continue to view it as more attractive on a relative basis given Corpay's solid earnings growth potential (15%-20% long-term earnings growth potential) and high-quality business model.

I continue to view Corpay's valuation as attractive relative to its own historical norms. As shown by the chart below, Corpay has historically traded at an average P/E ratio of 27x, which is well above the stock's current valuation. Additionally, Corpay's valuation remains reasonable relative to peers. Corpay continues to trade at a moderate valuation premium to WEX (WEX) which makes sense given WEX recent business challenges and slower growth profile relative to Corpay. Corpay continues to trade at a substantial discount to Jack Henry & Associates, despite the fact that both companies have similar near-term growth prospects.

Risks To Consider

The biggest near-term risk to the Corpay bull story right now is the macroeconomic environment. Continued uncertainty relating to the Trump administration's tariff policy has weighted on business confidence and could result in weaker than expected business spending for 2025. Additionally, fuel prices have moved sharply lower in recent weeks due to increased supply from OPEC and demand uncertainty due to the broader macroeconomic outlook. For these reasons, it could be challenging for Corpay to meet consensus EPS estimates for the rest of the year. That being said, Corpay is also well positioned to benefit from potential rising price levels due to elevated inflation more broadly due to tariffs. Overall, I expect the macroeconomic environment to improve as we move through 2025 and businesses get more certainty around U.S. trade policy.

I would view any move lower in Corpay shares due to macroeconomic weakness and potential earnings shortfalls as a buying opportunity as it does not change the long-term growth trajectory of the company as I view macroeconomic headwinds as short-term in nature and likely to reverse over the long term.

Conclusion

Corpay shares have slightly underperformed the broader market since my initial Strong Buy recommendation was published in January 2024. The company continues to deliver solid business performance driven by strong growth in its corporate payments business, which has reduced the company's exposure to the vehicle payments business.

I continue to view the company's capital allocation policy favorably and believe that a mix of debt reduction, share repurchases, and strategic bolt on acquisitions makes sense.

The stock continues to trade at a substantial discount to the broader market, despite having above market medium-term growth prospects. I am re-iterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock and believe Corpay shares represent a compelling investment opportunity at current prices.

data.title: 2 Attractive Income Plays Retirees Should Consider After Their Recent Sell-Off
data.description:
Introduction

What makes NNN REIT (NNN) and Ares Capital (ARCC) particularly attractive aside from their recent drops in price is their higher yields. And if you're a retiree, now's your chance to grow your income stream at a cheaper price.

Both stocks are particularly attractive for retirees, as you would be able to collect dividends inside a tax-sheltered account like a Roth IRA.

Moreover, retirees have less need for growth as a result of their age, instead relying more on income.

With both now trading at very attractive valuations, I discuss why retirees should consider both of these stocks for their portfolios.

Market Sell-Off

A couple of weeks ago marked the sell-off in the overall stock market. This was in anticipation of Donald Trump's Liberation Day where he implemented several tariffs on various countries.

Although the market somewhat sold off before, none could have anticipated what was in store after tariffs were implemented.

Subsequently, the Dow Jones Index (DJI) & S&P 500 (SP500) experienced a steep decline, over 2,300 points and 500 points respectively. As a result, this caused heightened fear in the market, resulting in a sell-off across nearly every sector.

Since then, the market has bounced back, with the Dow Jones & S&P both up closer to 3% at the time of writing. But the damage has already been done, with stocks suffering what I deemed as a much-needed sell-off.

The market has boomed the last 2 years, up over 20% in 2023 and 2024. So, it was only a matter of time before a sell-off occurred. As an income investor, I rejoiced as I was able to add to my existing positions at cheaper prices.

One thing to note is just because a stock has sold off and appears to be a bargain doesn't mean it can't get even cheaper. And with the trade war between the U.S. & China still ongoing, the market can see another sell-off in the near term.

But, this all depends on whether the two countries can come to an agreement. One thing is for certain, income investors should take advantage and continue dollar cost averaging into quality stocks that can likely continue paying dividends if we do see a recession.

That's why I'm recommending both of these stocks currently.

Why NNN REIT?

Aside from their 6% dividend yield due to the sell-off, NNN REIT isn't severely impacted by tariffs, making them and their peers attractive.

While they could see increased construction costs, their impact is minimal when compared to other sectors who import goods from outside countries.

Moreover, NNN REIT could see relief in the near term if interest rates are forced to decline as a result of a recession, which many are now calling for due to tariffs.

If one does come to fruition, then lower interest rates will benefit NNN REIT and the entire sector.

Higher interest rates impact REITs' cost of capital, compressing spreads, making investments less accretive.

So, if interest rates do decline, then REITs will likely see higher spreads, positively impacting their bottom lines.

NNN's management stated during Q4 earnings that they anticipate tighter spreads and pricing to compress in the coming quarters. But if interest rates are lowered, then NNN stands poised to benefit.

They would also get to refinance their upcoming debt at lower rates as well. NNN's 2025 & 2026 debt have weighted-average interest rates of 4.0% and 3.6%, respectively. But even if rates aren't lowered, their liquidity position was strong with $1.2 billion undrawn on their revolver.

They also retain ample capital after dividends. In 2024, NNN REIT had $196 million in free cash flow, up from $187 million in 2023.

So, should their bottom line become impacted by further tenant headwinds, NNN's robust liquidity, and 68.2% AFFO payout ratio gives them flexibility. And with 35 years of consecutive dividend increases, I expect their record to remain intact for the foreseeable future.

Speaking of tenant headwinds, which I discussed more in-depth last month, due to the REIT preferring to focus on non-investment rated tenants, a recession does serve as a serious risk.

Their tenants are smaller, lesser-known, and not as financially stable as retailers like Walmart (WMT) & Costco (COST), who Agree Realty (ADC) typically leases to.

But despite tenant headwinds which impacted NNN's occupancy, dropping year-over-year from 99.5% to 98.5%, NNN still managed to see solid AFFO growth of 2.8%. And for 2025 they are still expected to see some growth, albeit lower at 1.9%.

AFFO is expected in a range of $3.39 - $3.44, up from $3.35 in 2024. Investments are expected to be solid in a range of $500 million - $600 million for the year.

However, the macro environment could impact this going forward, ultimately suppressing growth, negatively affecting their share price.

But at a forward P/AFFO multiple of just 11.62x, NNN trades well-below their normal valuation of 15.59x. They also trade below their peer average of 13.86x, signaling they could be undervalued.

Moreover, if the FED does lower interest rates due to a recession, NNN's share price could be affected positively as REIT share prices typically move in the opposite direction of interest rates.

And despite NNN REIT not being considered a high-growth stock, their conservative payout ratio, robust liquidity, and low valuation make them a great pick for a retiree.

Why Ares Capital?

Ares Capital's, a somewhat riskier income play being a BDC, recent sell-off makes them attractive once again.

Due to BDCs lending to smaller, and sometimes financially distressed businesses, they are viewed as attractive for income investors & retirees, but riskier for growth, risk-adverse investors.

But why I'm recommending Ares Capital today, despite a recession potentially impacting their financials by causing rising non-performing loans and PIK income, is their conservative management team.

While many BDCs were rewarding shareholders with special dividends and increases, ARCC elected to hold their dividend steady at $0.48 since late 2022.

One reason could be their externally managed structure, but in my opinion, has more to do with their forward-looking management team. The last two years, ARCC prioritized growth instead of dividend raises, which positioned them favorably compared to peers.

Their top and bottom lines grew solidly year-over-year from $2.6 billion and $1.266 billion to $2.99 billion and $1.4 billion. Their double-digit bottom line growth resulted in net asset value growth from $19.24 to $19.89 per share.

So, while their portfolio may not be as defensively-positioned at 57% first-lien loans compared to their externally-managed peers like Blackstone Secured Lending's (BXSL) 98.7% and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending's (MSDL) 96%, ARCC's superior growth offsets this.

Additionally, their liquidity was robust, with $635 million in cash available and $5 billion capacity on their revolver. This is more than enough to satisfy their upcoming debt this year of $1.85 billion. Their leverage of just 0.99x was also well-below the peer average.

They also had a strong pipeline coming into 2025 with $1.2 billion in commitments, up 80% year-over-year, with a $1.8 billion backlog.

ARCC's non-performing borrowers have held up well in the past year despite higher for longer interest rates. These stood at 1.7% at cost and 0.9% at fair value, up from 1.3% and 0.6%, respectively.

And while these would likely rise in a recession, I expect ARCC to maintain their current dividend of $0.48, which they've paid for 15 years.

Ares Capital is also known to rollover ample spillover income into the upcoming year. At the end of this year, this stood at $922 million, or $1.37, roughly 3 quarters worth of dividends.

This could be used to make investments for growth, or for dividends should coverage become impacted from potential borrower loan defaults. And with ARCC's conservative management team and long track record, I suspect their dividend will remain safe.

Moreover, at a price of $19.99 at the time of writing, which they haven't traded near since late 2023, this gives the BDC a very attractive P/NAV ratio of roughly 1x. Wall Street also rates the stock a buy with a $23.35 price target.

While BDCs are seen as higher risk investments as a result of recession threats, I suspect volatility could increase in the coming months. Moreover, upside could be limited as the uncertainty surrounding interest rates due to recession fears.

If so, this will likely have a negative impact on BDCs. If rates remain higher with no additional cuts this year, I see upside for ARCC.

But as previously mentioned, ARCC's superior growth, ample liquidity, and share price below NAV makes them an attractive income play in either scenario. Additionally, their conservative management ensures their 9.7% yield will likely remain safe for the near to medium-term.

Bottom Line

Despite recession risks, which could cause pain & discomfort, both NNN REIT & Ares Capital are conservative with robust liquidity available that will likely ensure their yields remain safe, at least for the near to medium term.

Additionally, both have respectable dividend track records, 35 years and 15 years respectively, that I anticipate their management teams will work to ensure remain intact despite economic volatility.

Despite limited upside due to increased recession fears, both have strong investment-grade balance sheets and robust liquidity available to absorb the impact from distressed tenants should a recession come to fruition, or we see stagflation.

That's why it's imperative to focus on stocks with strong fundamentals who trade at attractive valuations to limit downside and continue to pay dividends to grow your income stream. Both NNN REIT and Ares Capital currently fit the bill.

data.title: Brinker International: Unjustified Sell-Off, Strong Comps May Continue
data.description:
Brinker International (NYSE:EAT) experienced strong growth over the past year as the company achieved record same-store sales growth of 31% for Chili's in Q2 FY25. EAT also achieved 600 basis points of operating margin expansion for the quarter.

While Brinker's achievements may not be as strong in future quarters in 2025, I do think that the company has a chance for further strong growth. The reason for that is because its Chili's restaurants comprise 89% of total company sales. Chili's restaurants are affordable options for dining out. So, I think the company can continue to achieve same-store sales gains as the year progresses.

The gains in future quarters may not be as high as in Q2 due to economic/market uncertainty, but Chili's does provide a good option for families to dine out without breaking the bank. Therefore, I think Brinker has a good chance to perform well in 2025.

I realize that Brinker's Maggiano's restaurants are a little pricier than Chili's, but Maggiano's only comprises 11% of total revenue. So, Maggiano's may not drag Brinker down that much if consumers trade down to more affordable options.

Brinker's Growth Catalysts

Brinker's recent success is largely due to the Chili's restaurants, which were ranked as the number one casual dining chain in the industry in 2024. This success was attributed to Generation Z, where sales among this group increased 23.5%. Brinker's emphasis on value probably led to these sales increases. Another factor could be the popularity of the Chili's Triple Dipper appetizer menu, which has been posted heavily on social media.

It is important to point out that Brinker bucked the trend of its industry over the past year. Chili's sales increased 8.9% in 2024 as compared to the industry's 3.1% decline. This demonstrates that Chili's resonates with consumers, probably due to its reasonable prices.

Brinker has plans to keep the positive momentum going, which may lead to strong performance for 2025. The company made recipe changes for a higher quality experience. This includes guacamole, which is freshly made each day, higher-quality chicken breasts, and by making its bone-in wings and bacon crispier. So far, EAT's improvements led to higher guest satisfaction scores.

The company also removed 13 menu items to focus on improving the existing options. Brinker also installed new kitchen display systems, which led to faster service for customers. Brinker is in the process of upgrading the remainder of its ovens to TurboChefs which accelerate cooking times. The TurboChef ovens yield crispier items such as ribs and quesadillas, save space, are more reliable and easier to clean.

Brinker made upgrades to the Maggiano's menu. This includes a freshly made Caesar salad, an upgraded meat sauce, a new Fettuccine Alfredo, a crispier chicken parmesan, a new meatball recipe, and a 30-layer meat & sausage lasagna.

Brinker also mentioned that Maggiano's has plans to improve its service efficiency and is evaluating its real estate. No specifics were given yet on these two initiatives.

Increasing margins are another growth driver for Brinker. The company increased its gross margin to 19.8% in Q2 FY25, as compared to 14% from Q2 FY24. The net income margin increased from 3.9% to 8.7% over the same time period. The margin gains were largely attributed to sales leverage from top line growth. Margin increases have a good chance to continue from Brinker's focus on more efficient operations, plus from more expected sales gains.

Brinker is expected to achieve total revenue growth of 18% for FY25, which ends in June, according to analysts' consensus estimates. This would result in total revenue of $5.23 billion for the fiscal year. EAT appears to be on the right track to achieve this with its positive sales momentum.

Valuation

Brinker is trading at 17x expected EPS of $8.35 for FY25 and at 15x expected EPS of $9.50 for FY26. This is much lower than the Restaurant industry's forward PE of 23x. Wingstop (WING) has a similar market cap and is overvalued with a forward PE of 63x.

Brinker had 20 upward earnings revisions over the past 3 months. Wingstop had 24 downward earnings revisions over the same period. Wingstop's earnings are expected to decline by -0.05% for FY25. Brinker is expected to grow earnings at an impressive 103% to 104% for FY25 and follow that up with a 14% increase for FY26.

Brinker's expected EPS of $8.35 for FY25 can be achieved with continued strong same-store sales to drive the expected total revenue growth of 18%. The strong margin increases that EAT has been achieving has the potential to drive earnings to hit the consensus EPS estimate.

Brinker looks like one of the better restaurant stocks in the industry in terms of a reasonable valuation and future expected growth. The company has been executing effectively, which has been reflected in its same-store sales and margin increases.

Technical Perspective

EAT's daily chart above shows the decline that the stock experienced in 2025. The good news is that the decline is not a result of any company-specific issue. The stock was just a victim of the market's broad sell-off. The other good news is that there is a bullish divergence between the price of the stock which declined from March into April while the RSI and MACD increased over the same period.

This looks like a bottoming of the stock. These set-ups typically result in a reversal for the stock back to positive from what I have observed. A new uptrend will be confirmed when the RSI moves above 50 and the blue MACD line crosses above the red signal line.

Brinker's Q3 FY25 earnings report scheduled for April 29, 2025 is the next likely catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting Brinker to achieve $1.38 billion in revenue and normalized EPS of $2.48 for the quarter. It should be interesting to see how same-store sales turns out. Another strong same-store sales increase would make it likely for Brinker to meet/exceed these estimates. The comps may not be as strong as they were in Q2, but they don't have to be that high to meet the estimates. They may need about 20% same-store sales gains to hit the estimates for Q3.

What is interesting is that Brinker stated in the Q2 earnings call question & answer session that the company is sustaining the high comps. So, it is possible that the company exceeds analysts' estimates in Q3.

Risks for Brinker

The expectations for Brinker are high for Q3 based on the success in recent quarters. It is possible that the company won't meet analysts' expectations for the next few quarters. Same-store sales growth could be significantly lower than recent quarters. Consumers could change their preferences to favor other restaurants or by preparing more food at home. This could have a negative impact on revenue and earnings and drive the stock lower.

High profile food poisoning incidents at Brinker's restaurants could lead to a decline in sales. There are various food-borne illnesses such as salmonella, norovirus, E. coli, etc. that could infect the company's customers. Negative publicity from these possible infections could cause consumers to avoid Brinker's restaurants.

Brinker has a high concentration of restaurants in certain states. Brinker has 18.8% of its restaurants in Texas, 11.8% in Florida, and 9.2% in California. Negative publicity, illness outbreaks, large natural disasters, or significant changes in consumer preferences in those states could have a large negative impact on sales.

Brinker's Long-Term Outlook

Brinker's turnaround has been highly successful. The company appears confident that the high same-store sales that were achieved in recent quarters can be sustained. If this is achieved over the remainder of FY25 and into FY26, the stock has a good chance to outperform many other restaurant companies in the industry.

The unjustified sell-off for the stock because of the broader market decline has the stock at a reasonable valuation. This leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock. Analysts have a one-year price target of $180 for Brinker. This would result in a 25% gain. That would take the PE to 19 based on expected EPS of $9.50 for FY26. That's still below the industry's average forward PE of 23.

data.title: Chevron May Pump Its Upside As It Becomes Cheap And Oversold
data.description:
Amid the volatility of energy commodity prices, some companies maintain stable performance in their upstream and downstream operations. One of these is Chevron Corporation (NYSE:CVX). As a dominant figure for over a century, it already knows how to stabilize production and margins by truly understanding market dynamics. So even if macroeconomic uncertainties persist, it remains strong with its effective business portfolio and inventory management. Moreover, its well-positioned balance sheet and high cash inflows can cover its capital-intensive business, debt, and dividends. These aspects can justify my bullish outlook on this stock.

Chevron’s Q4 2024: A Decent Run In A Volatile Market

Chevron Corporation has faced uncertainties amid the sticky inflation and geopolitical tension. Yet, its resilience and stable topline performance show its strong market positioning. This is reflected by its most recent performance. Its Q4 2024 operating revenue amounted to $48.3B, down by -1.2% YoY from $48.9B. Note that I excluded income from equity affiliates and other income to concentrate on its revenue from actual operations. We can point to the downward trend of both oil prices and demand.

Concerning the price, it experienced the unfavorable impact of oil price volatility. From Q2 to Q4, 2024, the price of WTI Crude oil per barrel fell from $87 to $71. Likewise, Brent dropped from $91 to $76. One thing that drove this was the increased oil output of non-OPEC producers, particularly the US. This affected Chevron, as it mainly derives its revenue from oil and gas production. As we can see, its net oil-equivalent production in the US Upstream segment rose from 1,598 to 1,646 MBOED.

Looking into the demand side, one aspect was the strong US Dollar Index or DXY, which rebounded and reached 108.5 at the end of 2024. This was also its highest value since November 2022. As a result, US-based products, including oil and gas, became costlier for other countries. This affected foreign demand for Chevron’s products.

We should also account for the stubborn inflation as it affected domestic demand across industries. One indication is the -64% US monthly import drop last October due to more products being rerouted to inventory restocking as spending softened.

Despite this, CVX enjoyed the strength of natural gas. Its realization or the price CVX received for the sale of natural gas rose from $7.31 to $7.67 in the international segment. In the US, it remained at $1.62, showing stability in the domestic market. This partially offset the impact of lower oil prices and demand.

Another thing I like about CVX is its strategic crude oil and other liquid inventory management. This is more evident in its international upstream segment throughout the year. And I believe it will always be one of its core strengths. So, I will discuss it more later.

I also think its prudent inventory management helped stabilize profitability. Its operating margin rose from 10.5% to 11.4% in Q4 2024. In addition, CVX became less cost-sensitive as its operating leverage decreased from 68.0% to 67.0%. This shows a lower variable cost contribution, which makes CVX more stable and resilient to inflation. I think this is an excellent aspect to note due to the highly volatile and cyclical nature of this business.

Laggards

Chevron is a dominant force in the oil and gas industry. Yet, it must watch out for market laggards that can affect its performance.

One is the offsetting impact of OPEC Output Hike and Trump’s tariffs. While oil output hikes can affect non-OPEC oil prices like that of Brent and WTI, new tariffs can be inflationary. This can be double jeopardy for CVX and other oil producers. Currently, Brent and WTI are close to $60, about 15-18% lower than their prices at the end of 2024. In addition, tariffs can affect consumption and production as it means higher import prices. These can affect many industries, including manufacturing, transportation, and logistics, which can decrease the demand for energy commodities. The question lies in whether exporters and affected importers will absorb these increased tariffs or pass them on to consumers.

Another challenge is the strong Dollar Index or DXY. This makes oil and gas exports less affordable in other countries. On a lighter note, it is slightly weaker today than in 2024. At $99.8, this may increase confidence in international markets. However, I don’t think it will last too long. If we look at its momentum using MACD and RSI, the dollar is already oversold. This can trigger a rebound in its value and index strength in the second half.

Drivers

These are things I believe will drive CVX’s growth and expansion and mitigate external risks.

Inventory Management And Increased Efficiency

In just a glance, it is easy to prove that Chevron understands market dynamics to stabilize its topline growth. Its prudent inventory management remains the cornerstone of its profitable operations. We can see how it adjusted its production to external factors, particularly in the international market.

As an example, DXY weakened from Q2 to Q3 2024. This could have increased oil and natural gas affordability and demand in other countries. In response, CVX increased its production from 1,720 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day or MBOED to 1,759 MBOED. When DXY rebounded in Q4, CVX scaled back its net oil-equivalent production fell from 1,794 to 1,704 MBOED. To be more specific, its liquid production decreased from 851 mbd to 834 mbd.

In the US, CVX increased its production, and I think that’s reasonable despite lower oil prices. After all, it has already acquired PDC Energy, which allowed it to expand in the Permian Basin. It also exceeded estimates on oil supply boost in the region. Another contributor is the strategic development of its Anchor Project in the Gulf of Mexico.

What I think is more interesting today is its advanced drilling techniques in the Permian Basin. With its triple-frac technique, it may bolster efficiency and lower production time and costs by over 10%. This method means CVX will fracture three wells in the region simultaneously to speed up its production by 25%. This will be applied to 50-60% of its Permian Basin wells this year versus 20% in 2024.

Solid Balance Sheet

I am impressed with its well-positioned Balance Sheet considering its high capital intensity. Its cash levels last Q3 and Q4 rebounded after the continued decline from 2022 to Q2 2024. At $6.7B, it is already 69% higher than its lowest point in 2024. As we can see, the company continues to generate positive free cash flows despite its continued expansion. With an FCF of $10.0B during H2 2024, it converted 9.8% of its total revenue into free cash. This is much better than the first half with an FCF/Sales Ratio of only 5.0%.

Receivables are also stable at $20.6B. If we combine their amount, they will comprise 66.7% of the total current assets. This shows that Chevron has enough true liquid assets. In addition, inventory levels are generally increasing but manageable, reflecting Chevron’s production boost in the Permian Basin. With a current ratio of over 1.05, CVX can cover all its current obligations.

Moreover, its debt levels remain stable at $24.5B, with mostly maturing over a year. To be more specific, 26.7% of its borrowings will not mature in five years. This shows that CVX has enough time to prepare for borrowing repayments. More interestingly, it has a very low Net Debt/EBITDA Ratio of 0.4x. This means that it has enough cash and earnings to pay all borrowings in less than a year.

Considering all this, CVX has a high capacity to sustain its expansion

Natural Gas Price Surge

For most parts, I talked about its crude oil business. So, I am concentrating on natural gas in this section. I expect it to become a key driver of Chevron despite campaigns for renewable energies.

At this point, I’m sure you know it helped CVX strengthen its topline performance. As discussed a while ago, CVX enjoyed a higher natural gas price realization, especially in international markets. Currently, it costs $3.53, 84% higher than its price a year ago. Despite its notable decrease from last month, I believe the price of natural gas will stay above $3.00 considering the heavy reliance of the power sector on it. We must also factor in the increasing LNG shipping capacity.

Even the US Energy Information Administration or EIA presents an optimistic outlook of its price at $3.80 this 2025, which is 20% higher than the previous forecast, and $4.20 in 2026. Although it projects its share of electricity generation to decrease to 39%, I believe it can’t be replaced by renewables. Above-average inventory withdrawals due to increased natural gas consumption can drive this.

Data Centers To Increase Natural Gas Demand

Another thing I believe will bolster natural gas demand and the price is the continued expansion of data centers around the world. Currently, they comprise 3% of the total electricity supply across the globe. This 2025, their electricity usage may stay above 500 TWh as estimated by Deloitte.

As the digital and AI revolution continues, data center investments and construction may speed up in the next few years. This is to accommodate the increasing internet and electricity use as household and business transactions and interactions become more digital and internet-driven. Amazon (AMZN), for instance, the leading e-commerce figure plans to allocate $100B in data centers to accommodate the increasing market demand. Hence, the number of data centers worldwide may rise from 6,111 to over 8,000 in 2030.

Does it mean anything for natural gas? Absolutely. Data centers rely heavily on electricity and electricity relies heavily on natural gas. Currently, natural gas comprises 43% of electricity share. So, data center expansion may require more natural gas. The additional 14 GW requires 2.22B cubic feet per day or Bcf/d of natural gas. After two to three decades, the natural gas requirements of data centers may reach 24.1 Bcf/d.

Amid these trends, policies to prevent natural gas shortages may be implemented. But I think market forces will naturally push its price higher, also raising CVX revenues from natural gas production. Lastly, new data centers may positively impact oil as they may require oil-powered generators to prevent disruptions in case of a grid failure or malfunction.

Price Ratio

CVX has increased dramatically since the pandemic. And I think that’s rational considering its key metrics, such as book value per share or BVPS and FCF per share. Currently, it trades at 1.58x its BVPS, which is lower than the five-year average of 1.66x. If we use this to derive the fair value, its TP will be $142.54, a 5.0% upside. Meanwhile, CVX appears much cheaper in FCF as it trades only at 16x versus the average of 30x. This gives us a TP of $249.98, an 83% increase.

To make a more logical observation, I also ran the data on a regression analysis. The correlation of the price versus BVPS at FCFS is 96%. This means that every time either of these two metrics changes, there is a 96% chance that the price will go in the same direction. In addition, both their p-values are very low with BVPs at 0.0007 and FCF at 0.001. This shows that less than 1% of their relationship in the past 20 years was random or happened by chance. This also erases any risk of biases in my analysis. Since BVPS has a lower p-value, it is better to assume that CVX is fairly valued using P/B TP.

It is important to note that these are historical values. Yet, the statistical findings are already solid clues to the potential price direction. So, they support my bullish rating of this stock.

DCF Model

I also used the DCF Model to have a more forward-looking analysis of CVX. Like the price ratios, it also suggests that CVX is underpriced today. The TP of $147.89 suggests a 9.0% upside potential.

To begin with, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to make it synchronized with the Fed’s target. After all, the oil and gas industry is highly sensitive to many external factors, including inflation. So, I think this is already conservative. Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. It’s easy to compute since almost everything is presented on financial reports and stock trading websites. I only set its risk premium at 5% to compensate for the risk to take in investing here.

Lastly, I computed its FCFF by projecting future amounts of net operating profit after tax or NOPAT, capex, depreciation, and net working capital. Then, I set them at present value to get the EV.

Technicals

It is true that not all dips are worth buying. Yet, CVX shows a lot of buying opportunities supported by its solid fundamentals. Currently, it is trading at its 52-week low and below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing negative sentiments recently. But the fact that the 200-day SMA is flat shows long-term price stability. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still above the 200-day SMA, indicating that a rebound is still possible.

This can be supported by MACD and RSI. While the momentum is still low, CVX is already oversold. So at its current price, buyers may find support as an entry point to push the price up.

Using a 1:1 Reward-Risk Ratio, we create a strategy by taking profit at $142.71. This is close to the P/B TP, supporting our bullish outlook again. Stop loss at $129.55 as touching this can lead to a downtrend continuation.

Conclusion

Chevron Corporation is a well-positioned company dominating the oil and natural gas exploration and production. It experiences various headwinds today, but its strategic inventory management, enhanced efficiency, and Balance Sheet can help it soften the impact. In addition, it has enough resources to take advantage of potential opportunities, especially natural gas and data centers. These can support my bullish view aside from the fact that is cheap and oversold. I would give this solid stock a strong buy rating.

data.title: Kingdom Capital Advisors Q1 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Fellow Investors,

With all major indices pulling back to begin 2025, KCA similarly suffered a 7% drawdown in Q1. Half of the drawdown was driven by the bankruptcy of Corsa Coal, which was about a 3.5% headwind for the quarter. Despite the failure of Corsa, we were still able to outperform our benchmark (Russell 2000 TR) and the Nasdaq 100 TR.

Kingdom Capital Advisors (KCA Value Composite) returned-7.08%(net of fees)in the first quarter, vs. -9.48%for the Russell 2000 TR,-4.27% for the S&P 500 TR, and -8.07% for the NASDAQ 100 TR. As a reminder, returns will vary by account due to rounding, account size, and timing of withdrawals or deposits during the year.

Our top contributors to Q1 returns were Superior Industries (SUP) and Intrepid Potash (IPI). Our largest detractors were Corsa Coal (CRSXF)(OTCQX:CRSXQ), Enviri Corporation (NVRI), and Venator Materials (OTC:VNTRF).

Liberation Day

Q1 was dominated by discussions of tariffs. Investors & international leaders are grappling with Trump’s current administration demonstrating more tariff follow-through than in his first term. With DOGE deployed to reduce the size of the government and Trump seeking to “balance trade,” markets are speculating on the long-term business impacts of these policy changes. The initial market verdict on the levies has been brutal. While macro topics aren’t my favorite, I can’t avoid discussing tariffs in this letter, but I will focus on how they directly impact our holdings.

The most acute pressure point remaining in our portfolio is Superior Industries International (SUP). As expected, they forecasted $160-180m of FY25 EBITDA in their March earnings release, despite anticipated declines in light vehicle production. Tariffs on U.S. autos are unsustainable in their current form. The new levies on Canada, Mexico, China, steel, aluminum, and finished vehicles would drastically raise prices and eliminate automaker profits. Analysis from the firm Wedbush noted this week that moving 10% of the current auto supply chain to the US would take three years and cost over $100B. But what company will undertake even a portion of that move when messaging changes weekly, daily, and hourly? I’m staying with Superior for a few reasons:

  • This administration has been adamant about lowering costs for consumers, which can be accomplished in the auto industry through deregulation and production increases. As they stand, the auto tariffs accomplish the opposite, so I anticipate tariff adjustments, exemptions, etc.

  • The US’s primary geopolitical opponent is China (not Canada or Mexico, I think?), and Superior is well-positioned to take market share as wheel demand moves from China to North America. Mexico was spared any further impacts on “liberation day” and will be a needed partner if we’re going to pull production out of Asia.

  • Protectionism in the EU is killing wheel demand from China (via Morocco), and Superior stands to benefit at their Polish facility.

  • Current tariffs aim to move finished vehicle production to the U.S., not necessarily USMCA-complaint parts. I significantly doubt Superior will face increased competition from suppliers moving inside the US.

If vehicle production drops sharply, Superior will need lender cooperation to amend leverage covenants. Admittedly, continuing to hold this position requires the belief that this administration will not push the auto industry into a multi-year winter. SUP mentioned “advanced negotiations” with TPG to settle their preferred stock on the March earnings call, and I expect this settlement will yield a pleasant surprise for investors. I hope this can be announced in time for Superior to be added to passive indexes on “rank day” (end of April), providing additional flexibility to deal with their debt. The ideal timeline would see Superior settle with TPG, run a sales process, and exit the public markets. Given the employment agreement updates in February, I expect that’s what they’re trying to do.

Our other positions are less sensitive to tariff impacts. Fortunately for Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP), you can’t import suburban office buildings. NLOP is marketing their best asset (on a $/sq ft basis) in Venice Beach, which was announced days before the end of Q1. I expect the Google office to sell for $50m or so, which will be enough to begin distributions to shareholders. NLOP also extended a lease with JP Morgan in Tampa that was previously set to expire, once again extending the runway of assets that were priced with no terminal value. One of the stated goals from recent interviews with Secretary Bessent is to reduce interest rates and deregulate small banks, which we hope will provide the juice NLOP needs to attract new buyers & financing for their remaining buildings.

We love the counter-cyclical exposure of United Natural Foods (UNFI) to grocery trade-down from restaurants. There are two potential tailwinds we believe the market is missing. One is proposed restrictions on SNAP spending on “junk” food, pushing $8B of monthly government assistance towards UNFI’s categories. The other is a looming contract expiration KeHe has with Sprouts Farmers Market, which covers about $2B of annual revenue. UNFI is the backup supplier and could stand to benefit from increased volumes if they can win more of the renewal. Even without these possible catalysts, UNFI is growing volumes and showing their turnaround is gaining steam. As they currently stand, tariffs on food imports and packaging will accelerate food inflation, which is beneficial for UNFI.

In our last letter, we noted Magnera (MAGN) could potentially benefit from tariffs. We were pleased with their February report, after which insiders stepped up and bought shares in the open market, including a $500k purchase from the new CEO. I consider Magnera a well-managed industry leader trading for an attractive valuation in a defensive category, trading for a trough multiple on trough earnings. We were very happy with their rollout as a public company, and we were not alone, as many notable funds have filed ownership of the stock on their latest 13F reports. Of note, we did buy some puts on the XLP ETF tied to this position, as it contains many of Magnera’s end customers. If those businesses weaken, we will have some protection.

Another tariff-exposed position is a.k.a. Brands (AKA), which sources most of their products from China. They issued guidance after initial Chinese tariffs were implemented (20% increase in duties on their products), and they could continue to be vulnerable to trade policy further escalating with China. What isn’t directly subject to trade policy is the continued demand for their trendy products, and even with some headwinds in Australia, they are expecting another year of double-digit US sales growth as they roll out more US stores. Every data feed I’ve checked shows continued acceleration in consumer demand despite fears about consumers at large. We’re planning to visit their flagship store in Vegas this month, so if you see me or Mike sporting some new streetwear in the next few months, you’ll know what happened.

While not an easy decision, we ultimately decided to sell Warrior Met Coal (HCC) during Q1 while we wait for the trade war to die down. China has already directly targeted US coal in their retaliatory tariffs, and with Warrior being 99% export exposed, we think there’s a decent chance met exports get worse before they get better. The US has proposed retaliatory charges on Chinese vessels, in the latest escalation of the direct impacts. Warrior remains the lowest-cost US producer, and we think they weather the storm, and respect how well Management has protected their balance sheet through the development of their new Blue Creek mine. I hope we get a chance to buy back these assets in the future with greater confidence. I think the latest round of tariff announcements will be devastating for the coal industry (as written) and want to wait before adding exposure.

We also reentered Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC), with the price finally dropping back to levels we targeted. This was coupled with a strong increase in natural gas prices, with the curve now fluctuating around the $4 mark. Unit has continued to impress us with their disciplined management of their liquidation, selling forward much of their expected gas production for the year above $4 near the peak of pricing in March. Their drilling rigs continue to generate hefty cash flow, and we estimate the ultimate liquidation value if the business remains around $400m, versus the current ~$200m enterprise value. Tariffs and recession fears have sent oil prices lower, but we’re happy to own a cashed up, sleepy Oklahoma operator with locked-in cash flows.

Other Positioning Updates

Getting zeroed on the Corsa bankruptcy filing was certainly not the start we wanted in 2025. Subsequent court filings have revealed missteps by management and lenders when working to refinance their debt. We were able to attract additional bidders into the auction process who confirmed our belief that the assets had significant potential, but we still fell short of a favorable outcome.

While not that exciting, we have parked cash into two SPACs during Q1, CLBR and OACC. Both trade near cash in trust, limiting their downside, and have some attached optionality. We like having cash exposure with upside in the event the market likes what the SPACs acquire. We expect investors watching Newsmax soar 2,000% after their IPO last week served as a catalyst for people to revisit this space.

A phenomenon like the Newsmax IPO provides a pretty good glimpse of the state of markets in 2025. People are extremely online. Volatility is high. Sometimes at KCA we take advantage of short-term dislocations in stocks, with a couple trades in Q1 that didn’t even last a day. But at a high level, we still think we are best served owning hard assets & businesses trading at a reasonable margin of safety to their intrinsic value. Cash flow doesn’t go out of style. You may have noticed us consolidating the number of positions we owned in Q1. In turbulent times, I think it’s best to keep a smaller number of names in the portfolio that we know best, making it easier to react to rapid news flow.

I remain focused on the small corner of the world where I have influence. Once we have more clarity on trade, I’m excited to pick up other stocks that have been hammered by the uncertainty. But for now, I’m happy to have higher levels of cash to wait it out. While we have generally avoided index-level hedges, we did decide to put some on during the recent phase of volatility. It’s not every day that the President of the US is tweeting videos saying he is intentionally trying to crash the stock market. I don’t pretend to know his end goals, but I am not going to ignore the signals being sent right now about the market at large. I feel good about what we own and feel better knowing we hold some protection against falling indices.

Business Update

We are, as always, extremely grateful to those of you that have referred potential investors to help us grow! While markets are turbulent, we know these are the exact times you all count on us to deliver returns that outperform the broader indices. We continue to publish research online, participate in podcasts, and network with like-minded managers in the quest for our next great investment. Mike and I plan to be in Vegas in a couple weeks for the MicroCapClub/Planet MicroCap combined conference. If you find yourself in the DC or Richmond area, we would love to see you.

Thank you for trusting us to steward your funds wisely. As always, reach out with any questions.

Sincerely,

David Bastian, Chief Investment Officer
